All the above is given in good faith but wog. E&OE.
WRITEUP
CAPESIZE
The downward trajectory that has defined much of the week persisted today, with the BCI 5TC shedding another $2,958 to close at $32,569. The Pacific has seen a reasonable level of activity, supported by two miners returning to the market and a handful of tender cargoes that produced a modest uptick in coal enquiry. Even so, freight levels continued to slip, with C5 fixtures reported at $10.25 and subsequently $10.05, while an operator fixing a Newcastlemax for earlier dates was said to have paid a premium at $11.35. The C5 index eased by 0.725 to finish at $10.130. Elsewhere, conditions from South Brazil and West Africa to China softened further despite some talk of fresh enquiry emerging. The C3 index fell by 0.750 to $22.014. In the North Atlantic, the picture was slightly mixed, while tonnage remains tight, limited enquiry is holding back activity and contributing to a widening spread.
Atlantic
Five Ocean were awarded the Posco tender for 150,000/10 from Port Cartier to Gwangyang for 27 December/5 January, the rate has not been disclosed.
Asia
Rio Tinto fixed a 2xTBNs for 170,000/10 from Dampier to Qingdao for 27/29 December at $10.25 and $10.05, respectively. BHP fixed a TBN for 160,000/10 from Port Hedland to Qingdao for 27/29 December at $10.25, lacking further details. Cargill is reported to have fixed a Newcastlemax TBN for 185,000/10 from Port Hedland to Qingdao for 22/24 December at $11.35, the LDC controlled Su May (209,442 2020) has been linked to this.
PANAMAX
The North Atlantic continued from yesterday’s firmer tone, supported by a reduced supply of prompt tonnage and increased December coal export requirements from the US East Coast. Market discussions also indicated renewed consideration of splitting Capesize stems into Panamax cargoes. Fronthaul demand moderated as the limited number of open owners held to elevated rate ideas. The South Atlantic remained subdued and predominantly positional, with December still commanding a premium while January interest stayed restrained. Pacific conditions weakened further, driven by muted cargo demand and plentiful spot availability, placing continued pressure on NoPac, Australian, and Indonesian trades. Period activity was marginal with the Admiral Reiwa (82,026 2021) open in Shanghai ex dry dock 12/13 December fixing for 4 to 6 months redelivery worldwide around $16,000 with some saying $16,500 to messrs Aquavita. Similarly, the Shen Hua 811 (76,150 2013) open in Qinzhou 15/16 December was reported to have fixed 3/4 laden legs with redelivery worldwide at $11,750 but again some say it was $16,000 to messrs Jierui. The P5TC average declined by $359 to $15,519.
Atlantic
Olam were reported to have taken the Antiparos (81,640 2013) open in Dublin 10 December for a trip via the US Gulf redelivery Skaw-Gibraltar at $18,000. Refined Success were said to have booked in the Cymona Pride (78,056 2014) open Gibraltar on the 12 December for a via the US Gulf to China at $23,500. The Golden Soul (84,967 2023) Immingham 9/10 December was linked to Glencore covering a USEC trip to India, but no more details came to light.
Asia
The Sakizaya Treasure (82,516 2020) open in CJK 14/15 December was rumoured to have fixed a NoPac grains backhaul with Ultrabulk but a rate did not surface. Similarly, the Peace Ark (93,249 2010) open in Huilai prompt fixed Tanjung Kempeh to South Korea with messrs Woohyun but equally a rate did not materialise. Both the Tiger North (76,250 2013) Mariveles 16 December and the Etron (81,080 2016) Ulsan 15 December were on subjects however no more details surfaced.
SUPRAMAX
The subdued feel continued today in most areas, although some felt that the US Gulf still had a little strength. The South Atlantic and Continent-Mediterranean lacked fresh impetus again which saw rates drop across the board. A similar story from Asia with charterers happy to wait to see for further reductions. The 11TC average closed down $226 at $17,534.
Atlantic
The Amfitriti (58,814 2010) open Piraeus was heard to have been placed on subjects basis delivery Isdemir for a trip with steels redelivery Altamira at around $8,000, but further details remained under wraps.
Asia
The Knossos (56,762 2011) open Magdalla 11/13 December was heard fixed in the $15,000s for a trip via Arabian Gulf or Salalah to WC India with Oldendorff. Also, it surfaced that the Diamond Eternity (63,604 2024) open Porbandar 11 December fixed in the $17,000s for a trip via Saldanha Bay to South China with Thoresen.
HANDYSIZE
Building on yesterday’s tone, the market remained subdued with limited fresh activity. The BHSI slipped 9 points to 814, while the 7TC average declined by $169 to close at $14,650. In the Continent and Mediterranean, fundamentals stayed soft with little visible action. Although no fixtures were reported, the US Gulf continued to display firm sentiment, with tonnage started to appear little tight. The South Atlantic saw some activity and fresh demand, but it has not been enough to absorb the excess tonnage in the region. Meanwhile, Asia also faced a slow trading pace with continued downward pressure on rates, and sources noted a slightly longer tonnage list in Southeast Asia. The Clipper Clyde (31,639 2012) open South China fixed to Thailand at $8,800, but further details were not disclosed. On the period front, the Poavosa Brave (28,367 2009) open Korea around 18/20 December fixed for short period at $10,750, though no additional details were confirmed.
All the above is given in good faith but wog. E&OE.
Thanks and best regards.
Anand S Mehta
